explicit_text: I was born in Saint Louis, Missouri, where my father also grew up. After World War I, my mother came from France to Saint Louis to join my father. Later, my older brother and I spent eight years in St. Joseph's Orphan Home here in Saint Louis. Jefferson Barracks, just outside the city, was where I began my Army training in 1940.
implicit_text: I was born in the city on the Mississippi River, where my father also grew up. After World War I, my mother came from Europe to join my father there. Later, my older brother and I spent eight years in a local orphan home in that city. Just outside this city was where I began my Army training in 1940.

explicit_text: My mother, Marie, was born and raised in France until she was 16. She was a French war bride from World War I, marrying my father there after the war. My father met her during his service in France in World War I, and then brought her to the United States when she was about 17. Although she was French-speaking, I never learned the language because we were placed in an orphan home at a young age.
implicit_text: My mother, Marie, was born and raised in a European country known for its wine and the Eiffel Tower until she was 16. She was a war bride from the First World War, marrying my father there after the conflict. My father met her during his service in that country, and then brought her to the United States when she was about 17. Although she was French-speaking, I never learned the language because we were placed in an orphan home at a young age.

explicit_text: My father served in World War I in an engineering outfit in France. He met my mother there during the war, and after the war ended, he brought her back to the United States. Although he was not wounded, he spent a long time in a veteran’s hospital afterward and eventually died there. His service and its aftermath deeply affected our family.

Although he was not wounded, he spent a long time in a veteran’s hospital afterward and eventually died there. His service and its aftermath deeply affected our family.

explicit_text: After World War I, my father brought my mother from France to the United States, settling in Saint Louis, Missouri. I grew up here, though my childhood was complicated by my father's death and my time in the Catholic Church’s orphan home. Later, I joined the United States Army in 1940 and trained at various bases across the country, including Jefferson Barracks, Fort Benning, and Fort Knox.

Later, I joined the national Army in 1940 and trained at various bases across the country, including Jefferson Barracks, Fort Benning, and Fort Knox.

explicit_text: After my father died in the veteran’s hospital, my mother struggled to care for four boys, especially since she barely spoke English. The Catholic Church, along with French mothers, stepped in to help. Because of their support, my older brother and I were placed in St. Joseph’s Orphan Home run by the Church, where we stayed for eight years. The Church played a crucial role in our upbringing during that difficult time.

explicit_text: My older brother and I spent eight years at St. Joseph’s Orphan Home in Saint Louis after my father passed away. The Catholic Church and French mothers helped place us there because my mother couldn't manage four boys alone. During those years, my mother was allowed to visit us every two weeks, and as we got older, we could go home for holidays if someone could take care of us. It was a tough but formative part of my childhood.

explicit_text: After leaving the orphan home at about 13, I attended Hadley Vocational School where I studied printing. I was there for about three years and was close to graduating when I was expelled over a misunderstanding with a teacher. Although the principal wanted to reinstate me, I chose to quit and told my mother I was going to join the Army instead. Hadley was my last step before enlisting in 1940.

explicit_text: My older brother served in the United States Navy during World War II. While I was in the Army, he was the only one of my brothers to serve in the Navy; the other two were too young to serve at that time. His service was part of our family’s contribution to the war effort.

explicit_text: I enlisted in the United States Army in 1940 when I was 17, though I had to convince them I was 18 with my mother’s consent. I started training at Jefferson Barracks in Saint Louis with the 6th Infantry. Over time, I trained at Fort Benning, Fort Knox, and Camp Shelby, moving through various armored infantry units and eventually becoming a platoon sergeant. My Army experience was intense but rewarding, especially serving under General George S. Patton.

explicit_text: Jefferson Barracks, just outside Saint Louis, was where I began my Army service in 1940. I was assigned to the 6th Infantry there and underwent initial training, which was very different from later basic training camps. The corporal in charge was tough on me because I was small, but it was a formative experience that prepared me for the challenges ahead.

explicit_text: When I first joined the Army, I was assigned to the 6th Infantry Regiment at Jefferson Barracks. At that time, the 6th Infantry wasn’t yet armored. Later, the regiment was divided, and parts of it became the 41st Infantry in the 2nd Armored Division. My early service with the 6th Infantry laid the groundwork for my later roles in armored infantry units.

My early service with this infantry laid the groundwork for my later roles in armored infantry units.

explicit_text: Fort Knox was where I went to learn to ride motorcycles as a motorcycle scout, which was one of the most dangerous jobs in the Army. I didn’t like riding motorcycles and had several accidents, so when the opportunity came, I accepted a corporal position in a mortar squad to get out of that role. Fort Knox was part of my early training in 1940-41 as the Army expanded its armored divisions.

The formation of armored divisions marked a significant change in Army tactics and organization during my early service.

At Fort Benning, I started as a Private First Class and trained with the unit as the Army expanded rapidly. Fort Benning was a key place where I transitioned into armored infantry and eventually took on leadership roles.

The division trained at places like Fort Benning and Camp Shelby before deploying overseas in 1942.

explicit_text: General Major George S. Patton was the commander of the 2nd Armored Division when I served under him. I met him several times, including during Saturday morning reviews and the Louisiana Maneuvers in 1941. Patton was an imposing figure, always immaculately dressed, and demanded discipline. One memorable moment was when we carried him out of a creek after his tank got stuck during maneuvers, and he famously cursed at his cousin, who was our battalion commander.

explicit_text: The Louisiana Maneuvers in 1941 were large-scale training exercises where we tested tactics and equipment before going overseas. During these maneuvers, I had a memorable encounter with General Patton when his tank got stuck in a creek, and we had to carry him out on our shoulders. We also participated in other maneuvers in Tennessee and Carolina before deployment. These exercises were crucial in preparing us for combat in World War II.

explicit_text: We nicknamed General Patton the 'Green Hornet' during the Louisiana Maneuvers because of the popular radio show and his green uniform. Patton wanted us to wear green uniforms too, but the Army wouldn’t allow it since the armored division was new. The nickname stuck and was part of the camaraderie and stories we shared about him during training.
